weeks.title: Week 1 — Backend testing: JUnit 5, Mockito, Spring slices
weeks.description: Make your Spring Boot code verifiably correct. Tests are what let you refactor fearlessly.

days.label: Day 1 · Monday
days.title: JUnit 5 fundamentals

tasks.text: Learn: test lifecycle — @BeforeEach, @AfterEach, @BeforeAll, @AfterAll
Learn: assertions — assertEquals, assertTrue, assertThrows, assertNotNull
Learn: @DisplayName for readable test names in the IDE
Learn: @Nested — group related tests inside inner classes
Learn: @ParameterizedTest with @ValueSource — run one test with multiple inputs
Write: first tests for a pure utility class (e.g. price calculation logic)

days.resource: Rule: A good test has one reason to fail. If a test checks three things, split it into three tests. Short, focused, named clearly.

days.label: Day 2 · Tuesday
days.title: Mockito — isolate and test service logic

tasks.text: Learn: @Mock creates a fake — replaces real dependency (repository, email service)
Learn: @InjectMocks — creates the class under test, injects @Mock fields into it
Learn: when(mock.method(arg)).thenReturn(value) — stub behaviour
Learn: verify(mock).method(arg) — assert a method was called with expected args
Learn: assertThrows() — test that your service throws the right exception for bad input
Write: ProductService tests — mock ProductRepository, test getById, create, delete
Write: JwtService tests — pure Java, no mocks needed. Test generateToken(), extractUsername(), and isTokenValid() for both a fresh token and a token with a different username

days.label: Day 3 · Wednesday
days.title: Test coverage — what to test and what to skip

tasks.text: Learn: test business logic, not framework code — Spring's save() doesn't need testing
Learn: test every branch — if/else, null checks, exception paths
Learn: JaCoCo coverage report — add plugin to pom.xml, run mvn test, open target/site/jacoco
Write: AuthService tests — test register (success, duplicate email), login (success, wrong password)
Write: OrderService tests — test placeOrder (success, empty cart, product out of stock)
Aim for 70%+ coverage on service layer — check JaCoCo report

days.resource: JaCoCo setup: Add jacoco-maven-plugin to pom.xml with prepare-agent and report goals. Run mvn verify, open target/site/jacoco/index.html in browser.

tasks.text: Learn: @WebMvcTest loads only the web layer — no DB, no full Spring context
Learn: MockMvc — perform("GET /api/v1/products").andExpect(status().isOk())
Learn: @MockBean — creates a Mockito mock and registers it as a Spring bean
Learn: jsonPath("$.content[0].name") — assert specific fields in JSON response
Write: ProductController tests — GET all (200), GET by invalid id (404), POST without auth (401)
Write: test validation — POST with blank name returns 400 with field error message

days.label: Day 5 · Friday
days.title: Integration tests with @SpringBootTest

tasks.text: Learn: @SpringBootTest loads full application context — slower but tests real wiring
Learn: @AutoConfigureMockMvc — get a real MockMvc that goes through full security filter chain
Learn: use H2 in-memory DB for tests — add H2 dependency with test scope
Configure: application-test.properties with H2 datasource, ddl-auto=create-drop
Write: full auth integration test — POST /register → POST /login → assert JWT in response
Write: product CRUD integration test — register, login, use token, create product, verify in DB

days.resource: Test pyramid: Many unit tests (fast, cheap) → fewer integration tests (slower, more realistic) → very few end-to-end tests. Don't invert this — it makes test suites painfully slow.

weeks.askPrompt: Show me how to write a Spring Boot integration test with @SpringBootTest, H2 in-memory database, and MockMvc — testing the full register → login → create product flow

weeks.title: Week 2 — Frontend testing: React Testing Library + Vitest
weeks.description: Test your React components the way users interact with them — not implementation details.

days.label: Day 1 · Monday
days.title: Setup Vitest + React Testing Library

tasks.text: Install: npm install -D vitest @testing-library/react @testing-library/user-event @testing-library/jest-dom jsdom
Configure vite.config.js — add test: { environment: 'jsdom', globals: true, setupFiles: './src/test/setup.js' }
Create src/test/setup.js — import '@testing-library/jest-dom' for custom matchers
Add test script to package.json: "test": "vitest", "test:ui": "vitest --ui"
Write a trivial first test — render a Button, assert it's in the document
Run npm test — confirm it passes and the test runner is working

days.resource: Why Vitest over Jest: Vitest is native to Vite — no extra config, shared vite.config.js, much faster. Same API as Jest so tutorials transfer directly.

days.label: Day 2 · Tuesday
days.title: Testing Library core concepts

tasks.text: Learn: render() — mounts a component into the test DOM
Learn: screen.getByRole, getByText, getByLabelText — prefer role queries (accessibility-first)
Learn: userEvent.click(), userEvent.type() — simulates real user input (better than fireEvent)
Learn: expect(element).toBeInTheDocument(), toHaveTextContent(), toBeDisabled()
Write: ProductCard tests — renders name, price, image alt text correctly
Write: test clicking "Add to Cart" button calls the handler with correct product

days.resource: Golden rule: Test what the user sees and does, not implementation details. Never test state variables directly — test the visible result of state changing.

days.label: Day 3 · Wednesday
days.title: Testing async components + mocking API calls

tasks.text: Learn: waitFor() — wait for async DOM updates after data loads
Learn: findByText() — like getByText but waits automatically (async-aware)
Learn: vi.mock('../api/products') — replace real API module with a mock
Learn: wrap components in QueryClientProvider wrapper for TanStack Query tests
Write: ProductsPage test — mock useProducts to return fake data, assert cards render
Write: test loading skeleton shows while isLoading is true
Write: test error message shows when query returns isError
Write: LoginPage test — type email and password, click submit, assert mutation called
Write: test form validation — submit empty form, assert error messages appear
Write: test password mismatch on RegisterPage shows correct error
Write: CartContext test — add item, assert count increments, remove item, assert gone
Write: test ProtectedRoute redirects to /login when user is not in AuthContext

days.label: Day 5 · Friday
days.title: MSW — mock the real API at network level

tasks.text: Install: npm install -D msw — Mock Service Worker intercepts fetch/Axios at the network layer
Create src/mocks/handlers.js — define handlers for GET /products, POST /auth/login etc.
Setup MSW server in test/setup.js — beforeAll(server.listen), afterEach(server.resetHandlers), afterAll(server.close)
Rewrite ProductsPage test to use real Axios instance — MSW intercepts and returns mock data
Test error scenario: override handler for one test to return 500, assert error UI shows

Write: full login flow integration test — MSW handlers for POST /auth/login and GET /auth/me, render App with MemoryRouter at /login, fill and submit the form, assert redirect to / and Navbar shows the user's name from /auth/me

days.resource: Why MSW: vi.mock() mocks the module — but MSW intercepts the actual HTTP request. It tests more of your real code (Axios config, interceptors, error handling) without a real server.

weeks.askPrompt: Show me how to set up MSW (Mock Service Worker) with Vitest and React Testing Library — including the handlers file and the test setup file, with one complete test example

weeks.title: Week 3 — Performance: backend and frontend
weeks.description: Find and fix real bottlenecks. Fast apps win users and clients.

days.label: Day 1 · Monday
days.title: Backend: the N+1 query problem

tasks.text: Learn: N+1 — fetching a list of 20 products triggers 21 queries (1 list + 1 per category join)
Enable SQL logging: spring.jpa.show-sql=true, spring.jpa.properties.hibernate.format_sql=true
Call GET /products — count the SQL queries in logs. More than 2? You have N+1.
Fix with JOIN FETCH: @Query("SELECT p FROM Product p JOIN FETCH p.category")
Fix with @EntityGraph: @EntityGraph(attributePaths = {"category"}) on repository method
Re-run the endpoint — confirm query count dropped to 1

days.resource: Warning: Disable show-sql in production — it floods logs and marginally impacts performance. Use it only during development investigation.

tasks.text: Learn: an index is a sorted lookup structure — makes WHERE and JOIN fast on large tables
Add indexes on columns used in WHERE/JOIN: category_id, created_at, name (if searched)
Learn: EXPLAIN SELECT ... in MySQL Workbench — reads the query execution plan
Insert 10,000 product rows with a SQL script — test query speed before and after indexing
Learn: never SELECT * in production queries — fetch only columns you need in projections
Use Spring Data JPA Projections — interface with only the fields needed for list views

days.label: Day 3 · Wednesday
days.title: Backend: Spring caching with @Cacheable

tasks.text: Add dependency: spring-boot-starter-cache
Add @EnableCaching to your main class or a config class
Learn: @Cacheable("products") on service method — result cached on first call, served from memory after
Learn: @CacheEvict("products") on create/update/delete — clears stale cache after mutation
Apply to: getAll categories (rarely changes), getProductById (frequently read)
Test: call same endpoint twice, watch SQL logs — second call should produce zero DB queries

days.resource: Default cache: Spring uses a simple ConcurrentHashMap by default. For production with multiple instances, swap to Redis. For now the default is fine.

tasks.text: Install React DevTools browser extension — open the Profiler tab
Record a session on the ProductsPage — identify components that re-render unnecessarily
Wrap pure child components in React.memo — prevents re-render when parent re-renders with same props
Wrap callback props in useCallback — stable function reference prevents child re-renders
Wrap expensive computations in useMemo — e.g. filtered/sorted product list derived from state
Re-run profiler — confirm the targeted components no longer re-render on unrelated state changes
Install: npm install -D rollup-plugin-visualizer — analyse what's in your bundle
Run npm run build, open stats.html — find any surprisingly large dependencies
Confirm all pages are lazy-loaded — each route is a separate chunk in the output
Convert large product images to WebP format — typically 30-50% smaller than JPEG
Add loading="lazy" attribute to all below-the-fold img tags
Run Lighthouse again — target 90+ Performance score on the live Vercel URL

weeks.askPrompt: Explain the N+1 query problem in Spring Boot JPA with a concrete example and show me three ways to fix it: JOIN FETCH, @EntityGraph, and a DTO projection query

weeks.title: Week 4 — Professional dev practices
weeks.description: Database migrations, API rate limiting, structured logging — things that separate junior from mid-level.

days.label: Day 1 · Monday
days.title: Flyway — database migrations

tasks.text: Add dependency: flyway-core — and remove ddl-auto=update from application.properties
Create src/main/resources/db/migration/ folder
Learn: naming convention — V1__create_users_table.sql, V2__create_products_table.sql
Write V1 through V5 migration files matching your current schema from scratch
Drop and recreate your dev DB — let Flyway rebuild it from migrations, confirm it matches
Write V6__add_product_slug_column.sql — add a new column to products, restart, verify applied

days.resource: Why this matters: ddl-auto=update is a dev convenience that silently breaks production. Flyway gives you versioned, repeatable, reviewable schema changes — the way every real team manages databases.

tasks.text: Audit all System.out.println() — replace every one with log.info() or log.debug()
Learn: log levels — ERROR (broken), WARN (suspicious), INFO (key events), DEBUG (verbose detail)
Learn: MDC (Mapped Diagnostic Context) — attach request-scoped data (userId, requestId) to every log line
Build: a logging filter that generates a requestId per request, puts it in MDC
Configure logback-spring.xml to output JSON format logs for production (easier to query)
Test: make a request, find the requestId in logs, trace the full request flow

days.label: Day 3 · Wednesday
days.title: API rate limiting + request validation hardening

tasks.text: Add dependency: bucket4j-spring-boot-starter — token bucket rate limiting
Build: RateLimitFilter — limit auth endpoints to 10 requests per minute per IP
Return 429 Too Many Requests with Retry-After header when limit exceeded
Review all DTO validations — add @Pattern for fields like phone, slug, URL
Add @Size(max=255) on all String fields that map to VARCHAR columns — prevent DB truncation errors
Test: hammer the login endpoint 15 times rapidly — confirm 429 after 10
Add @Email to all email fields and @Positive to price/stock fields across DTOs — catches malformed emails and negative numbers before they hit the DB

days.label: Day 4 · Thursday
days.title: Spring Boot Actuator + health monitoring

tasks.text: Add dependency: spring-boot-starter-actuator
Expose: management.endpoints.web.exposure.include=health,info,metrics
Visit /actuator/health — confirms DB connection, disk space, and app status
Visit /actuator/metrics — explore JVM memory, HTTP request counts, response times
Secure actuator endpoints: only expose health publicly, require ADMIN role for others
Configure Railway to use /actuator/health as the health check URL for the service

days.resource: Production tip: Railway and most PaaS platforms ping a health endpoint to decide if your app is ready to receive traffic. Actuator's /health is perfect for this.

tasks.text: Learn: main branch = production only. Never commit directly to main.
Learn: develop branch = integration branch where features merge before going to main
Learn: feature/* branches — create one per feature, merge to develop via PR when done
Set up branch protection on GitHub: require PR review before merging to main
Practice: create feature/add-product-slug branch, add the slug feature, open a PR, merge it
Learn: git rebase vs merge — use rebase to keep feature branch up to date cleanly

weeks.askPrompt: Show me how to set up Flyway database migrations in Spring Boot 3 — the Maven dependency, the migration file naming convention, and V1 through V3 migration SQL files for a users, products, and categories schema

weeks.title: Week 5 — Docker + GitHub Actions CI/CD
weeks.description: Containerise your app and automate testing and deployment on every push.

days.label: Day 1 · Monday
days.title: Docker fundamentals

tasks.text: Install Docker Desktop — confirm docker --version in terminal
Learn: image vs container — image is the blueprint, container is the running instance
Learn: Dockerfile — FROM, WORKDIR, COPY, RUN, EXPOSE, CMD instructions
Learn: docker build -t myapp . and docker run -p 8080:8080 myapp
Learn: docker ps, docker logs, docker stop, docker rm
Pull and run: docker run -p 3306:3306 -e MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD=root mysql:8 — your own local MySQL in a container

days.resource: Resource: docs.docker.com/get-started — the official tutorial is genuinely good and takes about 2 hours to complete.

days.label: Day 2 · Tuesday
days.title: Dockerise the Spring Boot backend

tasks.text: Build the JAR first: mvn clean package -DskipTests
Write a multi-stage Dockerfile: Stage 1 — Maven build. Stage 2 — slim JRE runtime only.
Use eclipse-temurin:21-jre-alpine as the runtime base — much smaller than full JDK
Build and tag: docker build -t shop-backend:latest .
Run: docker run -p 8080:8080 --env-file .env shop-backend:latest
Test: hit localhost:8080/actuator/health — confirm app inside container is healthy
Add a .dockerignore file — exclude target/, .env, .git, *.md so the build context stays small and fast

days.resource: Multi-stage benefit: Build stage can be 500MB+ (Maven + JDK). Runtime stage with just JRE + JAR is typically 150–200MB. Smaller image = faster deploys, less attack surface.

days.label: Day 3 · Wednesday
days.title: Docker Compose — run the full stack locally

tasks.text: Learn: docker-compose.yml — defines multiple services (backend, db) as one unit
Write docker-compose.yml: services for mysql (image: mysql:8) and backend (build: .)
Configure depends_on: backend waits for mysql to be healthy before starting
Configure volumes: persist MySQL data so it survives container restarts
Run: docker compose up — both services start, Flyway runs migrations, app is ready
Run: docker compose down -v — stop and clean up. Rerun up — confirm clean start from scratch
Add a healthcheck to the mysql service (test: mysqladmin ping) and set backend's depends_on to condition: service_healthy — backend now waits until MySQL is actually ready
Learn: GitHub Actions — automated workflows triggered by git events (push, pull_request)
Create .github/workflows/ci.yml in the backend repo
Write: on push to any branch — run mvn test with Java 21 and an H2 test profile
Write: on push to main — also build Docker image and push to Docker Hub or GitHub Container Registry
Add GitHub Secrets: DOCKERHUB_USERNAME, DOCKERHUB_TOKEN (never hardcode credentials in yml)
Push a commit — watch the Actions tab in GitHub — green means all tests pass

days.label: Day 5 · Friday
days.title: GitHub Actions — frontend CI + full CD pipeline

tasks.text: Create .github/workflows/ci.yml in the React repo
Write: on pull request — run npm ci, npm test -- --run, npm run build
Write: on push to main — run tests, then trigger Vercel deploy via Vercel CLI or webhook
Backend CD: on push to main (after tests pass) — Railway auto-deploys from GitHub (already configured)
Test the full pipeline: push a small change → CI runs → tests green → both apps auto-deploy
Add a README badge: ![CI](https://github.com/yourname/repo/actions/workflows/ci.yml/badge.svg)
Add dependency caching to both CI workflows — actions/cache for ~/.m2 (backend) and ~/.npm (frontend) — compare build times before and after

weeks.askPrompt: Show me a complete multi-stage Dockerfile for Spring Boot 3 with eclipse-temurin:21, and a docker-compose.yml that runs the backend and MySQL together with Flyway migrations

weeks.title: Phase 5 capstone — production-grade full-stack app
weeks.description: You've gone from zero to a professionally engineered, tested, containerised, CI/CD-deployed system.

projectCards.heading: What "production-grade" means now
projectCards.text: Your app is tested at unit, integration, and component level. The database schema is versioned with Flyway. Every secret lives in an environment variable. The codebase has no N+1 queries, rate limiting on auth endpoints, structured logs with request IDs, and a CI pipeline that runs tests on every push. Docker Compose lets any developer run the entire stack with one command.

tasks.text: All service classes have unit tests — JaCoCo shows 70%+ coverage
At least one integration test covers the full auth + product flow
React components have RTL tests — at least ProductCard, LoginPage, CartContext
N+1 queries eliminated — confirmed by SQL log inspection
MySQL indexes on category_id, name (or any searchable column)
@Cacheable on category list and individual product reads
Flyway migrations replace ddl-auto — schema is fully version-controlled
Rate limiting on /auth/** endpoints — 429 returned after threshold
Structured logging with requestId in every log line
Actuator /health configured as Railway health check URL
Git branching: main/develop/feature/* — branch protection on main
Docker Compose runs full stack locally in one command
GitHub Actions CI runs tests on every push — green badge on README
Lighthouse 90+ on Performance and Accessibility on live Vercel URL
Both repos have professional READMEs with architecture notes, setup guide, live URL
Self-assessment: without notes, explain Flyway migrations, the multi-stage Docker build, how GitHub Actions CI runs your tests, and how you found and fixed an N+1 query

weeks.askPrompt: Show me a complete GitHub Actions CI/CD workflow file for a Spring Boot 3 backend — running JUnit tests on every push, and building and pushing a Docker image to Docker Hub on merge to main
